speaker: interviewer
text: We're on tape 5 with Mr. Ervin Chaim Greenfeld, November 15, 1995. You were describing to me the horrors of Gunskirchen. So could you continue with that? Right before the liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Gunskirchen is a short but very sad story. As I said before, I think that the camp didn't last long. But it couldn't. I think, according to the records, out of 21,000 people, about 9,000 survived. Daily, 400 to 500 people died, mostly fall asleep or typhus or from-- I was one of the-- I don't know why, but I did survive it. Much better than me-- many, many more better and stronger than me couldn't make it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As I said, the food was just plain not around. We were going out to wait for a little water and prayed for a little rain. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I did see-- I did see a few of my close friends fall asleep. I mentioned that before. And I remember, also, that every-- the Friday night before the liberation, I was-- they asked me to daven. I was doing the prayers for a whole group of people. Among the people, there were some actors from Budapest. I'll never forget that. They tried to entertain the people. There's no food. There's no sleeping. So at least these people should be credited for what they did. Some actors-- I'll never forget it. They were singing over their beautiful melodies-- and try to act and cheer up these terrible people. And as I mentioned it, the physical-- the physical condition was nothing to talk about. But mentally, we were so dehumanized, like worms. And this, this is what I-- hurts me to think of them. This is the worst part of, of what I can think of. Because before, they did what they did to us physically, hurting us and beating us and starving us. They were dehumanizing us. Normal-- a normal human being couldn't do this, what they did to us-- to the-- to the-- to a mouse or to a rat, or to a-- to a-- to a worm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was the place, incidentally, where we did hear from Auschwitz. Somebody from our town [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wound up in Auschwitz. And he came and told us. Do you know what happened to the rest of your family? His name was Rhienits. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said, what do you mean? They all went up to-- in smoke. So people said, you see? You see what, what Flecktyphus does? We considered him-- that the man is off his mind. He's totally, totally crazy.

speaker: interviewee
text: He tried to talk about Auschwitz to us. We were never in Auschwitz. We were all in the labor camps on the Russian front and in Hungary, hard labor and hard work, and hard struggling. And-- but to put healthy people into gas chambers, this is the first time. And I am ashamed of it because, because we were told this by the Poles. We were told this many, many times before the war, before the war came to Hungary. And this Rhienits was talking about it. Sure, there was-- there were crematoriums. And there were chimneys with smoke going out. And everybody talked, said, this guy, this-- totally meshuggah. Unfortunately, he was not meshuggah. And it came the Friday afternoon. We heard shots a day-- a day-- a day before, or two days before. And we were told to stay inside, not supposed to go out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was a Friday afternoon, May 4th. The, the days were so monotone I don't-- that I cannot, cannot recall one day different than the other. I did see people standing in front of the latrine, getting into the bathroom, so-called bathrooms. And there was a German guard who was watching, who couldn't hold himself back. He was shouting-- him. This I do remember. But otherwise, the days were long and empty and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] monotone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Friday afternoon, I was davening. And a friend of mine, his name was Bitterman-- I think he's still around. All of a sudden, he drives a bicycle. And he comes in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he walks over. And he comes. And he says, the whole thing is over. And people got wild. People got wild. While this happened, probably a few people were crushed to death. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] While I was walking out, it was the first time I have seen human flesh being cut off from a body and being sold to, to, to, to-- for human consumption. And I was begging him, leave that body alone. They're going to bury him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- but the man was-- there was nobody to talk to. People were completely, completely not normal. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I also asked somebody else. He had to go out to the bathroom. I said, go further. Don't, don't you see? There's a body over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't know how to explain it. But that's the only explanation I have, that people were just plain not thinking. They, they were totally, totally-- they're-- they didn't function normally.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the liberation was on a Friday night. Everybody was running. And I put, put together a beautiful Seder I got from somebody. And I didn't get it from somebody. I just saw the man was dying-- died. And I put together a nice Seder. And I put together two pairs of tefillin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went in with this Bitterman, who was probably twice my age-- big, husky, strong man. And then everybody was running to the kitchen. And the kitchen was full of-- there were-- there were-- there were-- there were no Germans here. We didn't see American soldiers, either. But somebody said that the-- that the, the Germans disappeared. The Germans disappeared. It was-- it was enough for us to go around and look for something. And everybody's running to the kitchen. I think it was a dried fruit which I got, some kind of a dried fruit. And by the time I got to my place, I was the most unhappy. I was such terrible-- shocked. They stole my little [NON-ENGLISH], which I put together with two pairs of tefillin, including my father's and my friend's tefillin. And a beautiful Seder and a tallis, they took it. They told somebody-- somebody got so crazy, that he, he thought he's getting a big bargain, a big metsye.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was a beautiful bag, a German army bag. There was nothing in it. But it was very precious to me. But I lost it because people were, as I say, people were just not thinking rationally. There was no-- there was-- everything which is normal was not there. This was--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- this was Friday night-- Friday night-- late Friday night. And daven then-- and already was everybody was saying that tomorrow morning, the Americans are going to be here. Late at night, probably, they said that some Americans came down. But we-- I, I did not have any personal contact with them. Because we were-- because we were in a-- deep in a-- one of these barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see them the next day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. They came. And it was beautiful to see those beautiful, tall shoes, and, and the-- and the-- and the, the helmets. And I have a little story which comes a little bit later. It happened in Wels. But in about two days, we got together about three or four close people who did survive. One is Mr. Weiss, who is in Canada. I was actually like a child over there for many, many older people who lost their children. And so they considered me as theirs. And this Mr. Weiss was very close to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were walking out of the camp. And all of a sudden, I see a-- on a-- on a-- on a-- hanging on the clothes-- a hanging shirt out of the-- on the-- on the clothesline. And I, I said to the lady, Lady, can I have that?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, that's mine. I said, but I'm coming out from the camp. And I don't have anything decent. All right, take it. She didn't like it. But I took it. And we went in-- we were taken in-- we were taken in to a-- to a camp, to Wels. Wels is a bigger town. And we were taken over there to the big barracks of a camp-- of a-- of the army barracks. And many of them were carried by stretchers. Many of them were. I was-- thank goodness, I was on my own, with about five, six friends. We-- by the time, we got together. From my town, we were-- we were together, I would say, about 10, 12 people because-- from different barracks and recognized each other. So we stuck together. And was another, another bitter chapter of the-- of this liberation was that the American and the British Army, they did not know how to handle these, these skeletons. They didn't know how to handle it. I will never forget that they gave us-- brought us soup off the army barracks in a big, white [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dish. And by the time that they put down, it was hard. It was so much oil. They put in so much oil on top of it. People were dying-- diarrheas. People were terrible. And the place became dirty. They brought in German SSes. And they brought in Germans to, to clean up. The Americans were, in my opinion, very soft with these monsters. Because they should have-- they should have done to them what they did to us. They were very soft with them. I remember that they brought in some SSes from the camp. And one of the men, she said, no, you can do with him what you want. I said to myself, I'm going to-- there was one guy over there. He used a, a, a verse from, from, from Tehillim, from the Psalms, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. But he, he was kicking him. I didn't. I didn't, didn't have the strength, or, or not, not the ambitions. It was-- it was against my basic nature to go and kick a human being, even though it was animal-like, they were. In Wels, I-- God was with me, over there, too. What happened to me, I throw away all my clothing. And I got dressed [LAUGHS] in a SS uniform, in a SS uniform. It was the-- the hat was twice the size of my-- of my head, the hat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I'm walking on the street. All of a sudden, I see a whole group of people are coming toward me-- an American soldier. They caught some SSes. And he stops me. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]? He's a Jewish boy, a Jewish American soldier. And he wanted to find out if I'm a Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, yeah. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He said it to him in German that he should kiss my-- he should kiss [LAUGHS] my foot. I didn't-- I didn't understand what it was. I just saw that the SS was bending down and kissing my foot. This happened in Wels. Now, I have here a paper from Wels. I have-- which, which, which claims that I, I was there. But I was-- I was-- I was lucky to find a German-- an Austrian lady. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She took me to her house, an elderly lady. And she, she baked for me farina-- farina, not cooking, but baking it, that it should be probably the most healthiest thing for-- in my condition. I gave her meat conserves-- big cans of meat, which was poison for us. Unfortunately, not everybody realized it. And every day, I was-- and a-- and a-- and a plateful of cookies-- she prepared this to me every day. This was for about two or three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wels.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Wels.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wels is a bigger town. As an matter of fact, this same Wels-- I forgot to say this, but on the march from Mauthausen to Gunskirchen, we went through this town. Wels was the next town from Gunskirchen, maybe two, two kilometers from that. It's a-- it's a bigger city. And when we were marching to Wels, I saw that the crowd is going out. It was an air raid. And people were going out of the city when we were coming in. And I said to myself-- as I said it before too-- it would be the right thing to join them and walk with them and disappear. I didn't have the guts. I was afraid. And the way I looked, I don't know how I looked. But I didn't-- couldn't look too handsome. Probably hair-- I was very skinny. I have here a picture of-- later. But I was-- I wasn't-- I wasn't more than about 70 pounds at the time of liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Wels, after liberation, where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wels, unfortunately, in Wels itself, few of our friends, people-- 40, 45-- died from, from, from, from typhus. From-- they had the stomach typhus. They just couldn't take the-- or their body was totally ruined. Or they were taking something. You know, I remember, there was a Dr. Klein, who was a doctor from my town. And he says, I remember he was doing like this. And he says, he's brain-- he's already brain-dead. They had very high temperature, 46, which equals to-- in, in, in, in Celsius.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Fahrenheit, it must be 105, 106. Anybody with this high temperature-- well, over 40-- did not survive Flecktyphus. I was still very, very weak, I remember. And I, I registered that I'm going to Czechoslovakia. Because I figured, Czechoslovakia was still-- first of all, my town was part of Czechoslovakia. And it was-- Czechoslovakia was still on the better side. There was also more victimized than the Hungarian-- Hungary-- among.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this way, I think I'm going to get a chance to get home earlier. I didn't know what home waits for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I forgot to mention, that once, once I met a man who was together with my father during-- in his-- he was in his group. But when you came down to Mauthausen, there's so many groups got mixed up. And there's a Mr. Hershkowits. He says, I was together with your father. He's on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father? You didn't mention that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't-- I didn't-- I didn't mention-- I didn't mention it that he was a courageous man. And he stopped marching. And he was-- he had enough courage, in Hungary, to remain. And this Mr. Hershkowits says, your father's on the other side. And all of a sudden, I didn't know what the 'other side' means. Because today, on the 'other side' mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. OK, I apologize for the problem with the circuit breaker. And now that we've repaired the circuit breaker, I want to continue. You were talking about Mr. Hershkowits and your father. And telling you what happened there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I met this Mr. Hershkowits, who was actually a lantzman from my same town. He was my father's age. And his son was a close friend of mine. And it must have been in-- on the road around Mauthausen that I met many of these people who were actually coming from every part of the country. Because these, these labor campers-- there were hundreds and hundreds of groups here. But Mauthausen was a-- after this time of the game, it was already like the last time before the whole end of the show. So they were gathering from all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so it happened I met this Mr. Hershkowits. And he came to me. He says, your father is on the other side. And it scared me. What do you mean, 'other side?' 'Other side' means-- God forbid, somebody's not alive is also on the 'other side.'

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, no, he was a man of courage. And he had the courage, he had the-- he had the guts not to continue this, this idiotic march. He said-- he said, [SPEAKING HEBREW], which means in Hebrew, this is it. He wouldn't go further. And so God was good to him and to me. He spared him. Because I would-- I would say, that I was-- in our town, we had about 150, 160 families. My father was one of the few who did survive of his age. Because he's-- he, he wasn't an old man. He was 45. He was born in 1946-- he was 1896. So he was a young-- still a young man. But my friends-- most of my friends who did survive, they lost their father. So when my father passed away 26 years ago yesterday, I wrote to my brother, thank you, Almighty God, that He lended for us, for another nice couple of years. Because this was nothing but a gift, that he did survive. Because most of the-- most of people over 40-- even younger-- most of the young people didn't survive either. But especially older ones, didn't have a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we are back now in Wels. I just mention this because I don't have notes. And while I-- while I'm talking, there are-- I-- my, my thoughts are totally back over there in Austria. And every minute, something else might pops-- pops up in, in my memory. I'm surprised that I do remember what I do remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Wels-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Wels, we-- I registered that I'm a Czechoslovak. I thought I'm going to have a better chance to get home as soon as possible. And, and if I get home, what will wait for me at home, that was just a very, very hard secret, which nobody, nobody could guess. Everybody was-- as a matter of fact, I wrote a letter. I wrote a letter from Wels to my father. And he got it-- to Tornal'a. I'm just reminding myself that I did write a letter. And he knew that I was-- that I survived. I took my little package, whatever it was. But I noticed that-- and I went to the train, to Prague. But believe it or not, I was not strong enough to pick up my package on my shoulder. And I had to ask one of the-- one of the train men, he should-- [NON-ENGLISH], they call it-- he should help me up. I didn't-- I, I forgot my, my, my Slovak language that time. And I told him [NON-ENGLISH], he should help me to-- I should be able to step up. I was 17-- 16 years old when I couldn't-- I couldn't step-- I couldn't step up on the train. Over there, the steps are probably higher than what we have here. But sometimes, you have to make a step. I did not have-- I did not have any strength. He-- I ask him to help, help me up-- the conductor, conductor. And I came, too, with a friend of mine, with this same Mr. Krakowich, who was so painfully mourning after his brother-- the same car, Mr. Krakowich. We came down to, to Pressburg, Bratislava. That's the capital of Slovakia today, a old famous town, a holy town where our greatest rabbis were establishing, actually, the, the Jewish tradition for the whole Hungarian country-- Pressburg, Bratislava-- famous, beautiful town. And we stayed there not more than about 24 hours and tried to get a train further. We went down to Budapest. Budapest is also the first time I was there. He knew, already, the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took us into the so-called [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] big office of the-- of the-- of the-- of the-- of the Holocaust survivors. And you see over there, hundreds and hundreds of people with lists. Everybody looking for somebody. Everybody's looking for relatives, for friends, looking-- maybe you know where my brother is. Did you see my father? Did you see my sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first time-- I'll never forget that-- I come-- I came down to, to, to Pressburg. At the station, I met my former teacher, a lady, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Charlotte Brown. We were very close with the family. And she didn't see no man in the whole year, because the-- all these girls-- all the ladies were separated. The men were separated from the ladies. She saw me. And she hugged me and jumped on my-- like, like a mother finds a child. That's how she, she was-- so happy was she to find me. This was Charlotte Brown. She's-- she, she died, already, since then. And we went down to Budapest and registered over there. And I tried to find out about any of my father's-- anybody from my family. Unfortunately, I did find out that my mother and my three sisters, they just passed, passed away a few days before the liberation-- before my liberation. I was liberated on May 4. They were liberated April the 30th, in Bergen-Belsen. Bergen-Belsen is a story where I wasn't. But I read more than enough. Bergen-Belsen was liberated April the 30th. And my beautiful mother, who was a real beautiful woman, and my three sisters, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] they went through a hellish year. And my mother buried my oldest sister. And on the day of liberation, she was asking for water from some girls. By the time the girls came back, she closed her eyes. She was already gone. And my two other sisters, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they died two, three weeks apart. They just gave up the fight to live. They know-- they, they saw their mother dying. And my younger-- my youngest sister, Lilli, she was about 18. She just said, I'm not-- there's not-- no reason for me to, to, to, to get well. And I think she gave up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all this I-- all this I found out after I got home to Tornal'a. I got home to Tornal'a. It was a-- We're, we're going to pause now.
